coredata.dc:title: Remaining useful life prediction of lithium-ion battery based on chaotic particle swarm optimization and particle filter
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Li-Hua
Chen, Si-Jian
Shi, Ye-Fan
Peng, Ding-Han
Shi, Ai-Ping
coredata.dc:description: The remaining useful life (RUL) prediction of lithium-ion batteries is crucial to ensure the safety and reliability of electric vehicles. Due to the complexity of the battery aging mechanism, the accurate prediction of RUL by traditional methods is difficult to guarantee. To improve the prediction performance of the particle filter (PF), an improved particle filter based on the chaotic particle swarm optimization algorithm (CPSO-PF) is presented. Then it is applied to predict the RUL of lithium-ion batteries. First, for a better posterior estimate in the PF, CPSO is used to drive the prior distribution of the particles toward a high likelihood probability to obtain a better-proposed distribution, which helps overcome the problem of degeneracy and impoverishment of particles. Then, Three models were employed to track the degradation trajectory of the batteries, including PF、the extended Kalman particle filter (EKPF), and CPSO-PF. Finally, the RUL of lithium-ion batteries was predicted with the three models. The experimental results demonstrate that CPSO-PF has higher prediction accuracy and strong robustness.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Remaining useful life
Particle filter
Chaotic particle swarm optimization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flowchart of RUL prediction for model-based method.
The model diagram of the dynamic system space.
The framework of the CPSO-PF algorithm for RUL prediction.
Capacity prediction for battery #6 and its errors comparison.
Capacity prediction for battery #7 and its errors comparison.
The convergence curve of CPSO for battery #6. (a) Fitness values at 25th cycle; (b) Fitness values at 35th cycle; (c) Fitness values at 45th cycle.
The convergence curve of CPSO for battery #7. (a) Fitness values at 20th cycle; (b) Fitness values at 50th cycle; (c) Fitness values at 95th cycle.
Prediction results at different cycles for battery #6. (a) Prediction results at 25th cycle; (b) Prediction results at 35th cycle; (c) Prediction results at 45th cycle.
Prediction results at different cycles for battery #7. (a) Prediction results at 20th cycle; (b) Prediction results at 50th cycle; (c) Prediction results at 95th cycle.
The initial parameters for the model.
Capacity prediction of the batteries and its errors comparison.
Prediction results and evaluation indicators for battery #6.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Abbreviations: starting point, SP; prediction for the end of life, EoLpre; confidence interval, CI.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Prediction results and evaluation indicators for battery #7.

head.ce:title: Remaining useful life prediction of lithium-ion battery based on chaotic particle swarm optimization and particle filter

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Automotive and Traffic Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Automotive and Traffic Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Automotive and Traffic Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The remaining useful life (RUL) prediction of lithium-ion batteries is crucial to ensure the safety and reliability of electric vehicles. Due to the complexity of the battery aging mechanism, the accurate prediction of RUL by traditional methods is difficult to guarantee. To improve the prediction performance of the particle filter (PF), an improved particle filter based on the chaotic particle swarm optimization algorithm (CPSO-PF) is presented. Then it is applied to predict the RUL of lithium-ion batteries. First, for a better posterior estimate in the PF, CPSO is used to drive the prior distribution of the particles toward a high likelihood probability to obtain a better-proposed distribution, which helps overcome the problem of degeneracy and impoverishment of particles. Then, Three models were employed to track the degradation trajectory of the batteries, including PF、the extended Kalman particle filter (EKPF), and CPSO-PF. Finally, the RUL of lithium-ion batteries was predicted with the three models. The experimental results demonstrate that CPSO-PF has higher prediction accuracy and strong robustness.

ce:section.ce:para: Global climate change is posing a huge threat to human society. To realize the carbon neutrality goal, the transportation industry is carrying out low carbon transformation in various transportation channels ranging from the highway, railroad to shipping, etc. Transportation electrification has become an inevitable trend in the automotive industry. With the outstanding advantages of high energy density, high output voltage, long cycle life, low self-discharge rate, no memory effect, and wide operating temperature, lithium-ion batteries have developed rapidly in recent years [1,2]. Featuring nonlinear behavior and complex internal mechanisms, the lithium-ion battery operates as a dynamic and time-varying electrochemical system. As the number of charge and discharge cycles increases, the battery performance and lifetime will gradually degrade. The aging of the battery can be attributed to the following factors, including physical mechanisms and chemical mechanisms, such as thermal and mechanical stresses, side reactions, and complex operating conditions according to Ref. [3]. For instance, electrode material dissolution, electrolyte decomposition, solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film formation, overcharge, depth of discharge, and ambient temperature can lead to gradual degradation of battery performance, affecting the operational reliability and driving safety of electric vehicles. Therefore, it is a challenging task to monitor the state of health (SOH) and predict the RUL of lithium-ion battery accurately, which is crucial to ensure the reliable operation and timely maintenance of battery management system (BMS). For the prognostics of lithium-ion batteries, many valuable methods have been proposed by previous researchers. In general, it can be classified into three major types [4]: model-based methods, data-driven methods, and hybrid methods.

Model-based methods can be subdivided into electrochemical models, equivalent circuit models, and empirical models. In the model-based methods, the RUL of a battery is predicted via physical laws and equations describing how the battery capacity degrades or by constructing an empirical model to simulate the degradation occurring in the battery. For example, with the internal electrochemical reaction process of the battery, an electrochemical model is established, which is usually based on the reaction kinetics and porous electrode theory. Through circuit analysis, an equivalent circuit model is constructed, which characterizes the external characteristics of the battery via simulating the variation law of battery voltage, current, resistance, etc. An empirical model is fitted with measurement data, which usually uses different health indicators such as impedance, capacity, power, etc. to reflect the degradation state of the battery, this model establishes a mapping relationship between RUL and battery characteristics. Empirical capacity degradation models often use the exponential model [5,6], the polynomial model [7,8], the logarithmic model [8], the ensemble model [9], and the verhulst model [10].

Electrochemical models are characterized by high accuracy and generalization capability. Zhao et al.[11] reviewed the modeling methods for coupled chemical and mechanical behaviors of lithium-ion batteries at three different scales of particles, electrodes, and cells. To study the capacity degradation of lithium-ion batteries under cyclic charge-discharge conditions, Ashwin et al. [12] estimated the growth of the SEI layer using a pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) electrochemical model at different cut-off voltages and charging currents, the model integrates all the basic electrochemical processes inside the lithium-ion battery with a strong implicit algorithm, which features fast computational speed and robustness. In Ref. [13], an optimized degradation model based on the single-particle (SP) model is developed, the model can quickly and accurately predict the battery capacity degradation and voltage profile with the number of cycles and temperature, which also can be applied to online estimation. However, electrochemical models need more computational effort because of the more parameters and larger complexity. An insight into the complex reaction mechanisms of the physical and chemical reactions within the battery is an indispensable requirement for building electrochemistry models. Compared with electrochemical models, equivalent circuit models, and empirical models are relatively easy to build, but they are suitable for short-term state estimation and have weak generalization ability because the internal parameters vary with cycles. Shen et al. [14] estimated the capacity and maximum available output power of the system based on a second-order equivalent circuit model through the extended Kalman filter (EKF) and a recursive least squares (RLS) algorithm with a forgetting factor. It was validated in a practical BMS with good real-time performance and reliable estimation accuracy.

By capturing hidden relevance in large quantities of data, data-driven methods attempt to train a prediction model through statistical theory or machine learning without a mathematical model of the battery. And the data-driven model does not need to consider the complex mechanisms inside the battery and build a physical model. For example, Vidal et al. [15] reviewed battery state estimation methods based on machine learning methods such as feedforward neural networks (FNNs), recursive neural networks (RNNs), support vector machines (SVMs), radial basis functions (RBFs), and Hamming networks, and also stresses the importance of a large amount of high-quality training data to effectively train and validate the algorithms. Ren et al. [16] utilized a multi-dimensional feature extraction method with an auto-encoder to represent the degraded health of a battery, a deep neural networks (DNN) based on the RUL prediction model was then trained for the remaining useful life estimation of multiple cells. The results show that the method can effectively improve the accuracy of battery RUL prediction. Zhang et al. [17] proposed a long short-term memory (LSTM) recurrent neural network (RNN) to learn the long-term dependence between the degradation capacity of lithium-ion batteries, the neural network algorithm is able to accurately predict the RUL of the battery regardless of offline training data. While various data-driven approaches for the RUL prediction of lithium-ion batteries have proven to be reliable. Typically, data-driven models are sensitive to the training datasets, have strict requirements on data quality and quantity, and need to be adequately trained so that the deep learning model can extract the data features as fully as possible. Thus, its feasibility and practicality rely deeply on a large amount of reliable training data.

The balance between model complexity and prediction accuracy is still a challenge. In recent years, based on a combination of two or more methods, hybrid methods have been proposed to improve the accuracy of predictions with the strengths of each method combined. Xing et al. [18] proposed PCA-IGPR to perform RUL prediction of the lithium-ion battery, which is a model using principal component analysis fused with improved Gaussian process regression. The experiment results show that the PCA-IGPR had small prediction errors and good robustness. Zhang et al. [19] fused relevance vector machine (RVM) and PF to construct a battery aging model for predicting RUL. This hybrid model enables the training data to be reduced to 30 % of the overall degradation data, and can effectively improve the performance of battery RUL prediction. Zheng et al. [20] proposed a method combining the unscented Kalman filter (UKF) with relevance vector regression (RVR) to achieve battery RUL prediction. In Ref. [21], the advantages of PF and the bond graph (BG) model were combined to obtain higher accuracy and more precise confidence bounds than EKF. The Refs. [19–21] are based on filtering methods to build nonlinear degradation models from different perspectives, which have achieved good prediction results in the process of battery RUL prediction. In fact, filter-based methods belong to model-based methods, which are widely used for battery state estimation and remaining capacity prediction. Essentially, they are all models derived based on Bayesian estimation and can be specifically classified into two typical categories: Gaussian processes-based filtering methods and probability-based filtering methods. The Gaussian process-based filtering methods are mainly based on conventional Kalman filter (KF) models, including linear Kalman filter (LKF), EKF, and UKF. However, the Gaussian distribution condition limits the KF and its variants to be less applicable to the degradation process of a nonlinear, non-Gaussian system like a battery. To address this problem, probability-based filtering methods employ particle filters as general-purpose filters.

PF can provide a more accurate representation of the posterior probability distribution based on the observed and control quantities. The key is to find a set of random sample particles with associated importance weights to represent the posterior probability density. In Ref. [22], PF, non-linear least squares (NLLS), and UKF are compared, on three lithium-ion battery models (data-driven empirical model, equivalent circuit model, and physics-based SP model). The results show that PF has a more accurate prediction performance. Guha et al. [23] predicted battery RUL using an internal resistance growth model in the framework of PF and the prediction results and experimental data are in better agreement. In Ref. [24], an adaptive kernel-assisted PF method was proposed to increase the diversity between resampled particles, which resamples based on kernel density estimation and adaptively allocates appropriate kernel bandwidth to each particle. In Ref. [25], PF and support vector regression (SVR) were combined based on information fusion and applied to the estimation of battery capacity and RUL. In Ref. [26], ES-PF adopted an exponential smoothing algorithm to process the parameters of the battery model obtained by PF. The experimental results show that ES-PF has better RUL prediction performance than single PF. Chen et al. [27] utilized a grey neural network (GNN) fused sliding-window grey (SWG) model based on PF framework (GNN-SGMPF) for prediction of battery RUL, and it is demonstrated that the GNN-SGMPF has high accuracy and strong robustness on two types of batteries. Xue et al. [28] integrated a two-phase clustering scheme and a PF-LSTM learning algorithm based on PF and LSTM networks for long-term RUL prediction. The experimental results show that the hybrid method of PF-LSTM has good accuracy and robustness. Chen et al. [29] exploited a sliding-window grey model (SGM) and particle filter to obtain prominent performance in battery RUL.

Actually, there are two main problems with standard PF: (1) the phenomenon of weight degradation. After several iterations, except for a few particles with high weights, the other particle weights tend to be zero. There are two main solutions to this problem: resampling and selecting a suitable proposal distribution. (2) Sampling importance resampling (SIR) adds resampling to sequential importance sampling (SIS). Resampling solves the problem of weight degradation, but the low weights particles are ignored. In order to keep the number of iterated particles constant, the resampling process replenishes the discarded particles through the replication of the high weights particles by weight. However, in extreme cases, resampling will lead the particles to only one particular particle and all its replicas, causing serious particle impoverishment. In this regard, it is better to solve the problem of particle impoverishment by choosing a reasonable proposal distribution (also called the importance distribution), enriching the particle diversity, and improving the prediction performance.

In the current study, CPSO-PF is introduced into the RUL prediction of lithium-ion batteries. Compared with the traditional PF which uses the prior distribution as the importance distribution, CPSO-PF adopts the results of the CPSO as the importance distribution. Fig. 1 shows the flowchart of battery RUL prediction with an empirical model and filtering algorithm. The strengths of this research work are as follows:
(1)
The use of a logistic chaotic function to optimize the population particles of the swarm reduces the probability of the swarm falling into local extremes, which improves the quality of the initial solution;
(2)
The introduction of CPSO improves the particle filter algorithm, which alleviates the sample degeneracy and impoverishment;
(3)
The proposed method does not require the consideration of complex electrochemical reaction mechanisms, the establishment of accurate equivalent circuit models, or the collection of large amounts of historical battery data.

The remaining sections of this paper are organized as follows: Section 2 presents a theoretical and feasibility analysis of the particle filter algorithm and its improvements based on the chaotic particle swarm algorithm. Section 3 provides the battery aging data and the battery aging model. Section 4 conducts experiments on the capacity and RUL prediction of Lithium-ion batteries and analyses the results. Section 5 gives the conclusion.

PF has no specific requirements for the system state-space model. The model can be any equation and the noise distribution can be arbitrary. It is mainly composed of the following two equations:

k-1 of its previous moment, which has nothing to do with the state and observation at other moments and is also unrelated to the moment k. The observation z
k at any moment k relies only on the state x
k at that moment, with no relationship to other observations and states.

For non-linear and non-Gaussian systems, the likelihood function is often difficult to integrate mathematically, and a Monte Carlo sampling method can be used to approximate this Bayesian update problem numerically. According to the idea of Monte Carlo sampling, the probability density function (PDF) is approximated by a set of random sample particles with corresponding weights. Then the posterior probability distribution is calculated by these particles with weights:
(6)
p

|
z
1 : k

∑

; and
δ ( ⋅ )
is the Dirac function. If the posterior distribution
p
x k
|
z
1 : k
is known, the sampling particles obtained from this distribution are the most reliable, but in practice it is difficult to sample directly from this distribution. To solve this problem, it is possible to sample from the prior distribution or introduce a distribution with a known probability density function that is easy to sample, and this distribution
q

is called the importance distribution. Thus, the weights can be calculated by the following equation:

The core idea of PF is to draw a large number of random sample particles with corresponding weights from the importance distribution to stand for the posterior probability density. Therefore, the choice of importance distribution is truly significant. Usually, the prior distribution
p
(
x
k
|

k − 1

is selected as the importance distribution.

k i

Particle swarm optimization (PSO) algorithm is a typical swarm intelligence algorithm, which originates from the development of a group collaboration based on a stochastic search algorithm for the foraging behavior of a flock of birds. The core idea is, in the solution space, to use the information sharing between individuals in the population to make the motion of the whole population evolve from disorder to order, which is to find out the optimal solution. Each particle in the population stands for a potential solution to the optimization problem (fitness function).

Each particle has two properties: position and velocity. The particle position is a potential solution to the fitness function. By comparing information between the particle itself and other particles, dynamically changing the particle's velocity (direction of motion and distance) to follow the current optimal particle, and adjusting the particle's position to the optimal solution after a generation-by-generation search, the best value can be located in the solvable space eventually.

The update process of particle velocity and position in the standard PSO can be expressed as followings:
(16)
v
f , d
k + 1
=
v
f , d
k
+
c
1
r
1
(
p

k

f , d
k
)
+
c
2
r
2
(
g
f , d
k
−
x
f , d
k
)
(17)
x
f , d
k + 1
=
x
f , d
k
+
v
f , d
k + 1
Where,
f = 1 , 2 , 3 , . . . , F .
represents the number of population particles;
d = 1 , 2 , 3 , . . . , D .
denotes the dimension of the target search space;
c
1
and
c
2
are the learning factor, also called the acceleration constant; r 1 and r 2 are the uniform random number in the range of [0,1];
x
f , d
∈
[
−
X

max
]
denotes the position of the f
th particle in the population;
v

V

,
V
max
]
denotes the flight speed of the f
th particle. P and g represent individual and global extreme values, respectively.

PF in the resampling process, because the suboptimal function is used as the importance function that is
q
x k
|
x
k − 1
,
z
1 : k
= p
x k
|
x
k − 1
, there will be a problem that the predicted samples do not take into account the latest observations of the system state, which in turn leads to a large deviation of the generated samples compared with those generated by the true posterior probability distribution. PSO with its spatial optimization-seeking property is well suited for the improvement of importance sampling, making up for the shortcomings of the PF algorithm. As shown in Fig. 3, with PSO, the particle iterative optimization driving the particles to arrive at the high-likelihood probability region, a more accurate posterior probability density can be obtained, which optimizes the estimation accuracy.

PSO has the advantages of the general algorithm, independent of specific problems, easy implementation in principle, and memory ability. The optimization ability of the standard PSO algorithm relies mainly on the interaction and mutual influence between particles. The particles themselves lack a mutation mechanism. After the particles fall into local extremes, it is difficult to escape this constraint. If the above problems are encountered in the initial stage of initialization, it will lead to poor traversability of the algorithm. To solve the problem, the chaotic search function is introduced to optimize the search ability with the randomness, traversal and regularity of chaotic variables so that the particle swarms clustered in the local optimal are driven to the global optimal location. In this paper, a typical logistic chaotic search function is introduced:
(18)
s
k + 1
= μ
s
k
1 −
s
k
Where:
s
k
∈
0 , 1
,
μ ∈ ( 0 , 4 ]
are logistic function parameters; k is the number of iterations. When μ is taken as 4, the logistic chaotic function is in a completely chaotic state, and its randomness, ergodicity and regularity are the best, so μ = 4 in this paper.

The population particles of the swarm
x
f , d
k
are normalized to the chaotic variables
s
f , d
k
in the interval [0,1], and the chaos search is performed according to Eq. (19) to obtain
s
f , d
k + 1
, and finally the inverse normalization is obtained.
(19)
s
f , d
k + 1
= 4 ×
s
f , d
k
(
1 −
s
f , d
k
)

In this study, the sample particles of PF are used as the initial population particles of CPSO, and the fitness function is defined as Eq. (20), so the CPSO algorithm is integrated into the PF algorithm.
(20)

)

where R is the measurement noise variance, z
k is the latest observed value, z
k|k-1 is the predicted observed value.

To achieve the remaining useful life prediction, a chaotic particle swarm algorithm is used to iteratively find the optimal battery state parameters for the double exponential empirical model, and then the optimized battery capacity prediction is calculated according to Eq. (24). With the CPSO-PF algorithm proposed in this section and the battery ageing model established in Section 3, the specific steps to obtain the battery RUL prediction are as follows.

Set k = 0, initialize the particles
{
x
0
i
}
i
N
∼ p (
x
0
)
, sample from the initial distribution to obtain the initial state of each particle, and since the actual initial distribution is unknown, use the uniform distribution as the initial distribution; and give each particle an equal weight
w 0 i
= 1 / N
, then the initialized particle set
{
x 0 i
,
w 0 i
}
i N
can be obtained.

Sampling is performed based on the importance distribution
q
x k
|

k − 1
,
z
1 : k
to obtain the updated particles
{

k i
}
i N
.

Introduce CPSO according to Eq. (20) to calculate the fitness value of each particle, and update the velocity and position of each particle according to Eqs. (16–19) to drive each particle towards the high likelihood probability region to obtain particles that more closely resemble the true distribution.

Calculate the likelihood probability
p (
z k
|
x k i
)
of each particle and update the weights according to Eq. (12) and normalize the weights to
w ∼
k i
via Eq. (13).

If the number of effective particles
N e f f = 1 /
∑
i = 1
N
w ∼
k i
falls below the given threshold
N
t h r e s
=
2 3
N
, according to the magnitude of the normalized weights
w ∼
k i
, the particles with high weights
x ∼
k i
,
w ∼
k i
i N
in the particle set are copied and the particles with low weights are eliminated, and the weights of all particles are reassigned to 1/N.

The state estimation is performed according to Eq. (15); set k = k + 1, and if k ≤ k
max , continue with steps 2–6, then loop until no observations are updated. Eventually, the RUL is calculated based on the degradation prediction results and the failure threshold.

Fig. 3 shows the framework of the CPSO-PF algorithm for RUL prediction. The blue curve represents the probability density function at k + 1, and the red curve represents the probability density function curve at k + 2. First, initialize the set of particles to a state with uniform distribution and equal weights. According to the particle states (position and weight) at k, the initial prediction is carried out by PF, and then the position of particles is adjusted by the CPSO algorithm to converge to the region with a high probability density. Then it goes through the importance sampling process: Particles with higher weights are retained and particles with lower weights are discarded. Finally, a resampling step is introduced to fill up the number of discarded particles to ensure the constant number of iterated particles: The high weights particles are replicated by weight. Finally, the state at k + 1 is predicted based on the optimized particles. The above optimization steps are repeated to estimate the state at k + 2 until the end of the cycle.

Data from the NASA Ames Research Centre in the USA were chosen to verify the validity of the proposed method [30]. Specifically, battery labelled #6 and #7 was used. The battery was charged in constant current (CC) mode at room temperature at 1.5 A until the battery voltage reached 4.2 V. It was switched to charging in constant voltage (CV) mode until the charging current dropped to 20 mA. Then, it was discharged in CC mode at 2 A until the battery voltage dropped to 2.2 V. Finally, the charging and discharging cycles were repeated continuously to accelerate the ageing of the battery. When the rated capacity of the battery #6 drops to 1.6 Ah and the battery #7 drops to 1.52 Ah, then the battery #6 and #7 is considered to have reached the failure threshold.

In a trade-off between model accuracy and computational complexity, the double exponential empirical model was applied to fit the battery degradation process. And the initial values of battery #6 and battery #7 are shown in Table 1.
(21)
Q
k
=
a
k
exp
(
b
k
⋅ k
)
+
c
k
exp
(
d
k
⋅ k
)
where Q
k is the capacity at the k th cycle; and a
k , b
k , c
k and d
k represent model parameters, respectively; k is the cycle number; The initial parameter estimation can be computed with the cftool of Matlab.

The state transition and observation equation of double-exponential empirical degradation model can be written as follows:
(22)
x k
=
a k
b k
c k
d k
T

Where, x
k is the state vector; a
k and c

k is the capacity at the k th cycle; k is the number of cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation criteria for battery capacity prediction accuracy
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the performance of the methods. We define the following performance indices to quantify the accuracy of its tracking behavior. These indices are absolute error (AE), relative error (RE)、root mean square error (RMSE), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), and coefficient of determination (R2). RMSE is the standard deviation used to represent the difference between the predicted and observed values. MAPE is a measure of relative prediction error. R2 indicates how well the model tracks the observed values, and the closer its value is to 1, the better its performance is. The specific calculation formula is as follows:
(25)
A E =
| R U
L
p r e
− R U
L t
|
(26)
R E =
| R U
L
p r e
− R U
L t
|
/ R U
L t
(27)
R M S E =
∑
i = 1
k
(
Q
i
−
Q ˜
i
)
2
k
(28)
M A E =
∑
i = 1
k
|
Q
i
−
Q ˜
i
|
k
(29)
M A P E =
∑
i = 1
k
|
Q i
−
Q ∼
|
Q i
k
(30)
R
2
= 1 −
∑
i = 1
k
(
Q
i
−
Q ˜
)
2
∑
i = 1
k
(
Q
i
−
Q ¯
)
2
where k represents the size of the observation sample, Q represents the true capacity,
Q ˜
represents the predicted capacity,
Q ¯
represents the mean of the capacity observation sample, RUL
t represents the true RUL, and RUL
pre represents the predicted RUL.

To a certain extent, the predictive accuracy of battery capacity will affect the RUL prediction. Thus, before the battery RUL prediction process, it is necessary to predict the battery capacity decline trajectory. In this paper, PF, EKPF, and CPSO-PF were respectively used to predict the battery capacity. Fig. 4 and Fig. 5 respectively show the tracking performance of the three models on the battery capacity degradation trajectory.

According to the results shown in Figs. 4 and 5 for the battery #6 and #7, RMSE values of 0.0268, 0.0154, respectively, were obtained using PF, and 0.0253, 0.0175, respectively, were obtained using EKPF, and 0.02441,0.0126, respectively, were obtained using CPSO-PF. From the above error comparison, it can be seen that EKPF performs better than PF in predicting capacity for battery #6 but worse than PF for battery #7. This is due to the limitation of EKPF in dealing with nonlinear, non-Gaussian systems. For weakly nonlinear systems, EKPF can overcome the problems of classical particle filtering better, but for strong nonlinearization, the first-order linearization approximation leads to truncation errors in the model afterward, which makes the prediction performance of EKPF unstable. However, the proposed model has smaller errors than PF and EKPF in the capacity prediction of both batteries.

In addition, to further examine the performance of the CPSO-PF model in tracking the battery capacity decline, a method described in Ref. [18] was introduced for comparison (See Table 2 for detailed results). We should note that the RMSE and MAPE of the proposed model are larger than those of PCA-IGPR in both batteries. This is because RMSE and MAPE are more sensitive to some outlier anomalies, CPSO-PF does not capture the rapidly increasing capacity observation value during the cycles of battery capacity proliferation, but this will not affect its ability to track the long-term decline trajectory of the battery capacity, so the R2 of CPSO-PF is closer to 1 than that of PCA-IGPR. It can be revealed that the CPSO-PF model predicts the capacity of lithium-ion batteries with higher accuracy than the PF model and EKPF. Theoretically, this would also imply that the CPSO-PF model performs better than the PF and EKPF in the next RUL prediction.

The convergence curves of CPSO at different starting points of prediction are shown ( Figs. 6 and 7). Optimized via CPSO, six representative particles with less weight among sample particles were selected to shown their converge process, the fitness values of these particles all converged 35 steps before, indicating that the CPSO has fast convergence speed and good robustness.

Figs. 8 and 9 show the RUL prediction for battery #6 and #7, respectively. Table 3 and Table 4 show the RUL prediction results and their evaluation indicators with PF, EKPF, and CPSO-PF. The specific RUL prediction results are analyzed as follows:

From the analysis in Fig. 8 (a) and Table 3, we can see that the EoL
pre of PF was 85 cycles, RUL
pre was 60 cycles, the prediction error was 23 cycles, the relative error was 62.2 %, and the confidence interval was [36,160]. The EoL
pre of EKPF was 82 cycles, RUL
pre was 57 cycles, the prediction error was 20 cycles, the relative error was 54.1 %, and the confidence interval was [31,153]. The EoL
pre of CPSO-PF was 80 cycles, RUL
pre was 55 cycles, and the prediction error was 18 cycles, with a relative error of 48.6 % and a confidence interval of [38,153]. In Fig. 8 (a), though EKPF performed better than PF, the error accumulation caused by first-order linearization degraded the performance of EKPF for RUL prediction in the 35th and 45th cycle, with larger errors and wider PDF distribution than PF (Fig. 8 (b) and (c)). Yet, the prediction errors of the CPSO-PF model in the 25th, 35th and 45th cycles are smaller than those of PF and EKPF, and the PDF of RUL was also narrower and higher than that of PF and EKPF. We also found that the closer the prediction starting point is to the lifetime threshold, the higher the model prediction accuracy, and the narrower and higher the probability density function, which is probably due to the shorter step lengths that need to be predicted, reducing the model interference information for multi-step lifetime prediction.

To further verify the robustness of the CPSO-PF model, the RUL prediction was performed on battery #7 and the experimental results were consistent with the above analysis. The specific results and predicted data of battery #7 are shown in Fig. 9 and Table 4. Compared with PF and EKPF, it can be concluded that the RUL prediction error of the proposed CPSO-PF model is lower and the performance in RUL prediction is better.

In this paper, an RUL estimation method for lithium-ion batteries based on CPSO fusion PF was proposed. Firstly, a double-exponential empirical model was applied to establish the battery degradation model. To promote the accuracy and traceability of prediction, CPSO was used to improve the proposal distribution of particles by introducing the latest observations into the sampling distribution and optimizing the sampling process, driving the sampling distribution approximate the true posterior probability distribution for the better posterior estimation, thus avoiding the particle poverty phenomenon and improving the accuracy of state prediction. Then, the PF, EKPF, and CPSO-PF were used to predict the capacity of battery #6 and #7. The results show that the prediction performance of EKPF is unstable or even decreases when dealing with nonlinear, non-Gaussian systems compared to PF, while the CPSO-PF model proposed in this paper had a higher prediction accuracy with RMSEs of 0.02441, 0.0126, and MAEs of 0.01363, 0.0091, MAPEs of 0.00852, 0.0056. The RMSEs、MAEs and MAPEs of the proposed model in this paper are smaller. The RUL prediction error of the proposed model is also smaller for two different batteries with different prediction starting points. In summary, it can be obtained that the prediction of battery RUL based on the CPSO-PF outperforms the PF and EKPF. The CPSO-PF has the advantage of high accuracy and good robustness. It is of great significance for the health management of lithium-ion batteries.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was sponsored by the National Key Technology Research and Development Program of China (2019YFD1002500), Anhui Intelligent Electric Driverless Tractor Research and Development (HX20210387).
